frame_analyses.description: The first instrument enters the field from the lower left and begins engaging the needle-suture setup.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task timing starts here. Entry is controlled, but there is a brief setup interval before efficient progress begins.

frame_analyses.description: The trainee is advancing and pulling the suture through the marked Penrose site while the opposite instrument stabilizes the target.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle and strand control are acceptable, though there are a few extra regrasping movements.

frame_analyses.description: Both suture ends are free and the trainee is organizing the first knot over the repair site.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Transition from needle passage to knot tying is somewhat slow, with extra time spent arranging the tails.

frame_analyses.description: The first throw is being seated with a visible double wrap before tightening.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: This appears consistent with a surgeon's knot. The right-sided instrument appears to be the leading instrument.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is formed and tightened over the initial knot stack.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: A hand switch appears to occur here, with the left-sided instrument leading the single throw.

frame_analyses.description: The trainee resets the working end and begins the final throw configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Preparation is correct but a little time-consuming, with continued tail reorientation.

frame_analyses.description: The final throw is being seated over the existing knot stack.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The third throw appears to switch back to the right-sided instrument. The knot stack remains intact without obvious unraveling.

frame_analyses.description: The knot is complete and the trainee is separating and presenting the tails for cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: A notable amount of time is spent organizing the tails before the cutter is introduced and used.

frame_analyses.description: Both ends appear cut short and the instruments are withdrawing from the field.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: No drain avulsion or late knot failure is visible at task end.

knot_assessments.notes: A double wrap is visible before the first throw is seated, consistent with a surgeon's knot. The right-sided instrument appears to lead this throw.
The second throw appears to be a single throw with a left-sided lead. It seats cleanly without obvious backslip.
The final throw appears to switch back to a right-sided lead and remains intact after tightening. Exact hand leadership is moderate-confidence because of the off-axis monitor recording.

technique_summary: This is an overall competent run with controlled tissue handling, no avulsion, and no obvious knot failure. The stitch appears to approximate the marked site reasonably well and the knot stack looks stable, but efficiency is reduced by extra regrasping during knot organization and a prolonged tail-management period before cutting. Instrument control is generally steady, though the transition from needle passage to the first throw and the final cut sequence could be streamlined.
improvement_suggestions: After completing the stitch, organize both tails earlier and park the needle decisively so the first throw can begin without the extra setup delay seen here.
Reduce regrasping during throws 2 and 3 by maintaining clearer distinction between standing and working ends and keeping the active tail in a consistent working window.
Once the third throw is seated, move more directly to cutting by separating the tails vertically and bringing the cutter in only when both ends are already presented at a short, controlled length.
strengths: The suture bite appears close to the marked target area and the drain remains securely attached throughout the task.
The first throw appears to include a proper double wrap, and the subsequent throws maintain knot integrity without visible slip or unraveling.
Tissue handling is controlled overall, with no aggressive traction or obvious damage to the Penrose/drain construct.

rubric_assessment.overall_judgment: Good performance: technically correct, secure, and free of major failure. Score held back mainly by small placement error and efficiency losses during knot organization and final cutting, not by poor surgical control. With cleaner tail management and fewer extra regrasping movements, this same technical skill level could likely score noticeably higher on a repeat attempt.
biggest_time_losses: Suture cut phase at 45.1s — single biggest time sink; tail organization prolonged
Transition from needle passage into knot setup
rubric_assessment.uncertainty_notes: Confidence 0.67. Main uncertainty: off-axis monitor recording makes exact mm deviation and precise hand-leading interpretation harder.
